insight: When deciding whether to hold or attack with a Feral, check if both you and your opponent would kill the same unit (typically a Drone) if they breach. If yes, hold it. Also consider whether your opponent can replicate the breach threat next turn—if not, attack with the Feral.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion on optimal Feral decision-making in the midgame.

insight: A small 7-damage Antima into Arka is generally not worth pursuing because it leaves you severely over-defended relative to your opponent's likely economy position, and your opponent will be close to their own Arka by then, making the Arka ineffective.
author: consensus
context: Evaluating whether an aggressive Antima-Arka opener is viable.

insight: When going player 1 with a natural Blastforge on turn 3, Animus is often superior to Blastforge if you would otherwise waste the blue on turn 4 or 5. Third Engineer into Amporilla is a strong attacking progression in sets with those units.
author: p0lari
context: Critiquing an inefficient opening build order in a specific random set.

insight: In sets where Matrix is available alongside Amporilla, the set is defined by these two units: Matrix is one of the best defensive units and shuts down rushes, while uncontested Amporilla in a high-econ game is game-ending.
author: p0lari
context: Explaining why a particular game ended quickly despite both players' early decisions.

insight: Rhino has multiple legitimate use cases: as a gambit blocker against inefficient threat or when opponent has a valuable rearmost defender; as a first absorber in multicolor openings (bridge rhinos); and as a soak option when more efficient defense is unavailable. However, buying Rhino purely for soak when better alternatives exist is often a mistake.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion of Rhino usage patterns and when buying Rhino is justified.

insight: When using Rhino as a gambit blocker against inefficient threats like Frostbite, if your opponent spends multiple bites to make you block on Rhino instead of Wall, that is a favorable trade for you. If they bite the Rhino, you wasted nothing. If they don't bite, you can click it.
author: .bky_1556
context: Explaining Rhino's value against freeze-based removal.

insight: Against two Drakes, the general correct defense is one Forcefield plus one Rhino, rather than other combinations.
author: masn6811
context: Discussing Rhino's role in red mirror matchups.

insight: In endgame situations where you have spare resources and your opponent does not, and they are running low on soak/absorb, buying attack is virtually always correct even if the attack is inefficient (e.g., Rhino), because you want to end the game before running out of your own wall supply.
author: consensus
context: Discussing economy versus attack priority in late-game scenarios.

insight: Attack is more efficient than economy overall (especially Drones), but is limited by absorb barriers. Attack forces opponents to buy defense, which hurts their income. In mid-game, once attack begins putting on pressure and crosses the absorb barrier, it becomes more valuable than continuing to buy economy.
author: namington
context: Explaining the relative value of attack versus economy purchases.

insight: Proto (Protoplasm) is a better absorber than Wall on some defensive turns, making it preferable to buy in certain sets. In sets with good absorbers like Protoplasm, buying soak Rhinos is often a mistake compared to buying the unit with better absorb.
author: mrguy888
context: Reviewing a player's decision to buy soak Rhinos instead of utilizing better absorbers.

insight: In an arms race set (or sets with units like Protoplasm and Sentinel), buying drones early into attack is more efficient long-term than buying attack into drones. The sequence of drone→attack beats attack→drone in overall economy buildup.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussing economy sequencing in arms race contexts.

insight: In base+Apollo+Scorchilla, Tarsier is likely stronger than Scorchilla because: (1) Scorchilla is more vulnerable to Apollo than Tarsier, making red players reluctant to buy them versus Apollo-based opponents; (2) Scorchilla is not significantly better than Tarsier in a pure red mirror to justify detouring to green for a Conduit.
author: consensus
context: Analyzing hypothetical set composition of base + two specific units.

insight: Fully synced Scorchilla attacks (holding Scorchillas to attack simultaneously every other turn) are less efficient than semi-synced (mixing Scorchilla and Tarsier). While synced Scorchilla denies absorb for 2/3 of turns, the value lost from delaying multiple Scorchillas outweighs this benefit around the 3-5 Scorchilla range.
author: .bky_1556
context: Debating optimal Scorchilla synchronization strategy in base+Scorchilla.

insight: With synchronized Scorchilla attack, base+Scorchilla (which lacks good nonprompt soak) benefits from the timing defense provided by synced damage, making semi-synced Scorchilla stronger than previously believed. The cost of holding Scorchillas is offset by superior defensive positioning.
author: mtanzer
context: Reconsidering Scorchilla synchronization after practical testing.

insight: In Ebb Turbine sets, P1 should generally open DD/DD/DDB rather than DD/DB/D1 (turbine on turn 3), even though the latter provides better drone-selling capability. The one gold difference outweighs the blue advantage, and you can click Ebb every turn with optional drones as needed.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussing optimal P1 opening sequencing with Ebb Turbine available.

insight: With one Ebb Turbine, you can click it every turn without issue. With two Ebb Turbines, you will only want to click one per turn, forcing you to float resources, which is inefficient. A second Ebb should only be bought if you have a concrete plan (e.g., Evermore, Cauterizer, or Comet) to justify the floated resources.
author: consensus
context: Explaining when multiple Ebb Turbine purchases are justified.

insight: Never buy three Ebb Turbines—this is a near-universal rule in Prismata. While rare exceptions exist, they are so uncommon that new players can safely ignore this possibility.
author: masn6811
context: Providing heuristic advice for new players on Ebb Turbine purchases.

insight: Specific Ebb Turbine opening sequences for P1: DD/DB/DEb is almost never correct. Prefer DD/DD/DDB/DCEb or DD/DDE/DDD/DDDB instead, which provide better economy and tech timing.
author: masn6811
context: Providing concrete opening line recommendations for Ebb Turbine contexts.

insight: Mahar Rectifier is approximately at par (baseline efficiency) for medium-sized attackers in Prismata. Some players use more or less efficient units as their par definition, but Mahar Rectifier is a common reference point.
author: .bky_1556
context: Establishing unit efficiency baseline standards.

insight: Hound (presumably a unit) is better than Mahar Rectifier as an attacker, but sometimes the engineer attached to it doesn't generate value. However, Mahar Rectifier's 2/5 body makes it very efficient for defending, offsetting Hound's superior attack role.
author: masn6811
context: Comparing two medium-sized attackers and their flexibility.

insight: Frostbite is an inefficient threat. When your opponent uses Frostbite, the cost-benefit analysis depends on how many bites they spend: if they spend bites to make you block on suboptimal units instead of optimal ones, you are ahead if they spent multiple bites.
author: .bky_1556
context: Evaluating defensive strategy against freeze-based control.

insight: When your opponent has very valuable rearmost defenders (like units that absorb effectively), using Rhino as a gambit blocker can force them to not click absorb, saving you multiple points of defense value. Combined with Feral, this is even more powerful.
author: .bky_1556
context: Explaining Rhino's value in protecting valuable defenders from absorb.

insight: When playing Gauss Cannon versus Immolite, Gauss Cannons win specifically against P1 T1 Animus into Immolites. The Gauss player can pick off each wave of Immolites as they come off cooldown while also damaging drones, while the Conduit player uses Forcefield to block new Immolites and buys Engineers to stabilize economy.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussing a specific matchup between two early-game strategies.

insight: When deciding whether to accept a gambit (attack offer while opponent is undefended) with 3-4 Drakes: if you can easily rebuy Blastforges while buying Shredders and Pixies with your blue resources, take the gambit. However, Pixie and Shredder/Infusion in the random set make not offering the gambit correct, as they make defending easier for the opponent.
author: iminabearsuit
context: Evaluating gambit decisions in specific board states.

insight: When clicking attack units, consider what you'll do with your remaining resources next turn. If you're about to buy a Blastforge and realize you won't have blue to spend next turn, don't buy the Blastforge now—save the resources for next turn's more efficient purchases.
author: p0lari
context: Teaching lookahead planning to evaluate whether purchases are efficient.

insight: Holding drones is likely better than holding Splitter in situations where you only have poor units to buy with gold and your opponent is having difficulty defending. Drones are only as good as what they buy; attack has higher value when defense is strained.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussing hold priority when facing resource scarcity.

insight: Holding Splitter is usually better than holding drones in most scenarios, as Splitter provides immediate tempo/attack value.
author: mrguy888
context: Contrasting default hold priorities between two units.

insight: Hannibull (Hannibull) is relevant in low-economy games where the goal is to keep the opponent from ever reaching 7 attack to activate it. Two damage for 7BR is significant value, making Hannibull rush strategies legitimate in such contexts.
author: consensus
context: Discussing Hannibull's clickability and rush viability.

insight: When going Hannibull, it's sometimes worth allowing your opponent to kill 2 Engineers and a Drone to get one click, because once you have Hannibull absorbing, those Engineers don't matter for defense anyway. This makes the Hannibull strategy easier to execute by keeping your opponent away from 7 attack.
author: masn6811
context: Explaining sacrifice efficiency in Hannibull rush strategies.

insight: In green-focused sets, P1 can mirror P2's natural Conduit third Engineer build with: DD, DD, DDCE, DDC1, DD1A or DD1B. This is half a turn slower on secondary tech but gives P1 a 1/3 turn advantage in droning. However, this is less efficient than DD/DDE/DDD and leaves you floating resources or wasting energy.
author: .bky_1556
context: Analyzing P1 opening options in Trinity Drone contexts.

insight: The natural high-economy Conduit opening (DD/DDE/DDD/DDDC) leaves P1 up one gold and one drone compared to forced third Engineer lines, while being down one Conduit. This is generally more efficient than alternative opening sequences.
author: p0lari
context: Comparing opening efficiency in high-economy Conduit contexts.

insight: In Trinity Drone sets, Trinity's main advantage for P2 is enabling slightly higher economy than normal without committing to a full third Engineer, with the natural Conduit acting as an extra drone equivalent early while enabling turn 4 Animus followup.
author: p0lari
context: Explaining Trinity Drone's role in P2 economy strategies.
